corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: Generalizable, transferrable, and robust representation learning on graph-structured data remains a challenge for current graph neural networks (GNNs).

context_window.previous: Graph Contrastive Learning with Augmentations Yuning You 1* , Tianlong Chen 2* , Yongduo Sui 3 , Ting Chen 4 , Zhangyang Wang 2 , Yang Shen 1 1 Texas A&M University, 2 University of Texas at Austin, 3 University of Science and Technology of China, 4 Google Research, Brain Team {yuning.you,yshen}@tamu.edu, {tianlong.chen,atlaswang}@utexas.edu syd2019@mail.ustc.edu.cn, iamtingchen@google.com
context_window.current: Generalizable, transferrable, and robust representation learning on graph-structured data remains a challenge for current graph neural networks (GNNs).
context_window.next: Unlike what has been developed for convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for image data, self-supervised learning and pre-training are less explored for GNNs.

top_candidates.sentence: In this section, we compare our proposed (self-supervised) pre-training framework, GraphCL, with state-of-the-art methods (SOTAs) in the settings of semi-supervised, unsupervised and transfer learning on graph classification (for node classification experiments please refer to Appendix G).
We first evaluate our proposed framework in the semi-supervised learning setting on graph classification on the benchmark TUDataset .
Generalizable, transferrable, and robust representation learning on graph-structured data remains a challenge for current graph neural networks (GNNs).
In this paper, we propose a graph contrastive learning (GraphCL) framework for learning unsupervised representations of graph data.
In this paper, we propose a graph contrastive learning (GraphCL) framework for learning unsupervised representations of graph data.
We first design four types of graph augmentations to incorporate various priors.

The results show that, even without tuning augmentation extents nor using sophisticated GNN architectures, our GraphCL framework can produce graph representations of similar or better generalizability, transferrability, and robustness compared to state-of-the-art methods.

The results show that, even without tuning augmentation extents nor using sophisticated GNN architectures, our GraphCL framework can produce graph representations of similar or better generalizability, transferrability, and robustness compared to state-of-the-art methods.
Numerous variants of GNNs have been proposed to achieve state-of-the-art performances in graph-based tasks, such as node or link classification , link prediction and graph classification .

As to the conjectured reasons for the lack of GNN pre-training: first, real-world graph data can be huge and even benchmark datasets are recently getting larger ; second, even for shallow models, pretraining could initialize parameters in a "better" attraction basin around a local minimum associated with better generalization .
It is thus difficult to design a GNN pre-training scheme generically beneficial to down-stream tasks.
It is thus difficult to design a GNN pre-training scheme generically beneficial to down-stream tasks.
Therefore, a well designed pre-training framework is needed to capture highly heterogeneous information in graph-structured data.
Self-supervision with handcrafted pretext tasks relies on heuristics to design, and thus could limit the generality of the learned representations.
If extended to pre-training GCNs, this framework can potentially overcome the aforementioned limitations of proximity-based pre-training methods .

Then a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) is adopted for the graph-level downstream task (classification or regression): Various GNNs have been proposed , achieving state-of-the-art performance in graph tasks.

In this section, we compare our proposed (self-supervised) pre-training framework, GraphCL, with state-of-the-art methods (SOTAs) in the settings of semi-supervised, unsupervised and transfer learning on graph classification (for node classification experiments please refer to Appendix G).
Dataset statistics and training details for the specific settings are in Appendix E.
We first evaluate our proposed framework in the semi-supervised learning setting on graph classification on the benchmark TUDataset .
We adopt graph convolutional network (GCN) with the default setting in as the GNN-based encoder which achieves comparable SOTA performance in the fully-supervised setting.
